This approach results in a robust, data-efficient, and reliable calibration solution, proving particularly effective in practical scenarios where collecting extensive calibration data is a dominant constraint. Our experiments show that the uncertainty for the 3D predictions is higher closer to the cameras. The data points in $uv$-coordinate space are more sparse in that region, even though they are not in 3D space. This work is relevant for anyone who is tasked with the calibration of complex multi-camera systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a GP-based implicit multi-camera calibration method that learns 2D-to-3D mapping with inherent uncertainty quantification and active learning for data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Conventional explicit calibration methods struggle with complex non-linear distortions, and neural network-based implicit methods are data-hungry and lack uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Uses Gaussian Process regression to learn 2D image coordinates → 3D world mapping directly, bypassing explicit intrinsic/extrinsic parameter estimation, integrated with Active Learning for data-efficient sampling.
AI.result: Uncertainty for 3D predictions is higher near cameras due to sparser uv-coordinate data points in that region, despite density in 3D space.
AI.conclusion: GP-based implicit calibration with UQ and Active Learning provides robust, data-efficient, and statistically reliable calibration for complex multi-camera systems.

title: How Far Is Document Parsing from Solved? PureDocBench: A Source-TraceableBenchmark across Clean, Degraded, and Real-World Settings
summary: The past year has seen over 20 open-source document parsing models, yet thefield still benchmarks almost exclusively on OmniDocBench, a 1,355-pagemanually annotated dataset whose top scores have saturated above 90%. Athree-stage audit pipeline we run on OmniDocBench screens its 21,353evaluator-scored blocks and confirms 2,580 errors (12.08%); combined with overa year of public availability, both annotation quality and contamination riskcall its rankings into question. To address these issues, we presentPureDocBench, a programmatically generated, source-traceable benchmark thatrenders document images from HTML/CSS and produces verifiable annotations fromthe same source, covering 10 domains, 66 subcategories, and 1,475 pages, eachin three versions: clean, digitally degraded, and real-degraded (4,425 imagestotal). Evaluating 40 models spanning pipeline specialists, end-to-endspecialists, and general-purpose VLMs, we find: (i) document parsing is farfrom solved: the best model scores only ~74 out of 100, with a 44.6-point gapbetween the strongest and weakest models; (ii) specialist parsers with <=4Bparameters rival or surpass general VLMs that are 5-100x larger, yet formularecognition remains a shared bottleneck where no model exceeds 67% whenaveraging the formula metric across all three tracks; (iii) general VLMs loseonly 0.99/8.52 Overall points under digital/real degradation versus 4.90/14.21for pipeline specialists, producing ranking reversals that make clean-onlyevaluation misleading for deployment. All data, code, and artifacts arepublicly released.

AI.tldr: PureDocBench is a new programmatically generated document parsing benchmark with 1,475 pages across 10 domains, revealing that SOTA models score only ~74/100, specialists rival larger VLMs, formula recognition is a shared bottleneck, and clean-only evaluation is misleading for real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks like OmniDocBench suffer from annotation errors (12.08% error rate) and contamination risks due to public availability, making current rankings unreliable.
AI.method: PureDocBench is programmatically generated from HTML/CSS with verifiable source-traceable annotations, covering 10 domains/66 subcategories with 1,475 pages in clean, digitally degraded, and real-degraded versions (4,425 total images). 40 models evaluated across pipeline specialists, end-to-end specialists, and general VLMs.
AI.result: Best model scores ~74/100 with 44.6-point gap; small specialist parsers (≤4B params) rival 5-100x larger VLMs; formula recognition <67% across all models; VLMs lose only 0.99/8.52 points under degradation vs 4.90/14.21 for pipeline specialists.
AI.conclusion: Document parsing remains unsolved, clean-only evaluation is misleading, and degradation robustness differs significantly between model architectures.

title: DIMoE-Adapters: Dynamic Expert Evolution for Continual Learning in Vision-Language Models
summary: Continual learning enables vision-language models to accumulate knowledge and adapt to evolving tasks without retraining from scratch. However, in multi-domain task-incremental learning, large domain shifts intensify the stability-plasticity dilemma. Most existing methods rely on fixed architectures with statically allocated parameters, which limits adaptation to new domains and aggravates catastrophic forgetting. To address these challenges, we propose DIMoE-Adapters, a Dynamic Incremental Mixture-of-Experts Adapters framework that introduces a dynamic expert evolution paradigm to balance stability and plasticity. This paradigm is implemented through two collaborative components: Self-Calibrated Expert Evolution (SCEE) and Prototype-Guided Expert Selection (PGES). SCEE constructs and evolves a sparse expert pool through expert optimization dynamics, improving plasticity while reducing redundant capacity. PGES controls expert utilization based on the pool shaped by SCEE, improving stability across both previously encountered and unseen tasks. Extensive experiments show that DIMoE-Adapters outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods across various settings.

AI.tldr: DIMoE-Adapters uses a dynamic Mixture-of-Experts framework with self-calibrated expert evolution and prototype-guided selection to balance stability and plasticity in continual multi-domain task-incremental learning for vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Existing continual learning methods rely on fixed architectures with statically allocated parameters, which limits adaptation to new domains and aggravates catastrophic forgetting, especially under large domain shifts in multi-domain task-incremental learning.
AI.method: Proposes DIMoE-Adapters with two components: (1) Self-Calibrated Expert Evolution (SCEE) that dynamically constructs and evolves a sparse expert pool, and (2) Prototype-Guided Expert Selection (PGES) that controls expert utilization for better stability across seen and unseen tasks.
AI.result: Outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods across various settings in multi-domain task-incremental learning.
AI.conclusion: The dynamic expert evolution paradigm effectively balances stability and plasticity, reducing catastrophic forgetting while enabling adaptation to new domains.

title: Lightweight Unpaired Smartphone ISP Transfer with Semantic Pseudo-Pairing
summary: Unpaired smartphone ISP is a challenging problem due to the lack of scene and color alignment between RAW and target RGB images. Many existing methods either require paired data or rely heavily on adversarial training, which can become unstable in the unpaired setting. In this work, we present a simple and effective approach developed for the NTIRE 2026 Learned Smartphone ISP Challenge with Unpaired Data. Our method first reconstructs larger images from training patches to recover global context. Then, we extract semantic embeddings with DINOv2, and use fused Gromov-Wasserstein (FGW) optimal transport to build pseudo pairs between RAW and RGB images at both image and patch levels. This semantic matching allows us to partially alleviate the unpairedness of the data and build these pseudo input-target pairs. Based on these pseudo pairs, we train a lightweight CNN with only 7K parameters for color rendering. The network is designed to be compact and focus on color transformation rather than structural change, which helps reduce artifacts and improve training stability. Our challenge submission achieves 22.569 PSNR, 0.675 SSIM, and 8.067 $ΔE$ on the final hidden test set, significantly improving over the baseline and achieving the 3rd best SSIM and $ΔE$ among all challenge entries. Our code is available at github.com/nuniniyujin/Unpaired-ISP .

AI.tldr: The authors tackle unpaired smartphone ISP by using DINOv2 semantic embeddings and Fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport to create pseudo pairs from unpaired RAW/RGB data, then train a tiny 7K-parameter CNN for color rendering, achieving strong results in the NTIRE 2026 challenge.
AI.motivation: Unpaired smartphone ISP is challenging due to lack of scene and color alignment between RAW and target RGB images; existing methods require paired data or rely on unstable adversarial training.
AI.method: 1) Reconstruct larger images from training patches for global context. 2) Extract semantic embeddings using DINOv2. 3) Use fused Gromov-Wasserstein (FGW) optimal transport to build pseudo pairs at image and patch levels. 4) Train a lightweight 7K-parameter CNN for color rendering on these pseudo pairs.
AI.result: 22.569 PSNR, 0.675 SSIM, 8.067 ΔE on hidden test set; 3rd best SSIM and ΔE among challenge entries.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semantic matching via FGW optimal transport effectively mitigates the unpairedness problem, and the compact CNN focuses on color transformation with improved stability and fewer artifacts.

title: Cloud-top infrared observations reveal the four-dimensional precipitation structure
summary: Accurate four-dimensional (4D) precipitation information is essential for understanding the Earth's energy and water cycles, yet remains observationally unresolved at global scales. Conventional theory holds that geostationary infrared observations primarily sense cloud-top properties, with limited sensitivity to sub-cloud precipitation. Here we show that cloud-top infrared measurements nevertheless encode sufficient information to recover the four-dimensional structure of precipitation, revealing a previously unexploited observability of sub-cloud processes. We introduce a physically constrained deep learning framework, 4DPrecipNet, in which a moisture-first constraint requires the latent representation to recover precipitable water vapour, anchoring the model in thermodynamic consistency. By integrating multi-channel infrared radiances with these constraints and radar-derived precipitation profiles, we reconstruct the vertical and temporal evolution of precipitation systems from geostationary orbit. The framework captures deep convective structures and their evolution, with robust performance across large samples and independent radar comparisons. These results demonstrate that sub-cloud precipitation is physically encoded in cloud-top infrared observations, establishing a new pathway for continuous global monitoring of precipitation structure.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces 4DPrecipNet, a physically constrained deep learning framework that shows cloud-top infrared observations from geostationary satellites can recover the four-dimensional structure of precipitation, challenging the conventional view that such data have limited sensitivity to sub-cloud precipitation.
AI.motivation: 4D precipitation information is essential for understanding Earth's energy and water cycles, but remains observationally unresolved at global scales. Conventional theory holds that geostationary infrared observations primarily sense cloud-top properties with limited sensitivity to sub-cloud precipitation.
AI.method: The authors introduce a physically constrained deep learning framework called 4DPrecipNet, which incorporates a moisture-first constraint requiring the latent representation to recover precipitable water vapour for thermodynamic consistency. It integrates multi-channel infrared radiances with constraints and radar-derived precipitation profiles to reconstruct vertical and temporal evolution of precipitation systems.
AI.result: The framework captures deep convective structures and their evolution, with robust performance across large samples and independent radar comparisons. It demonstrates that sub-cloud precipitation is physically encoded in cloud-top infrared observations.
AI.conclusion: Cloud-top infrared measurements encode sufficient information to recover the four-dimensional structure of precipitation, revealing a previously unexploited observability of sub-cloud processes. This establishes a new pathway for continuous global monitoring of precipitation structure.

title: Diffusion-APO: Trajectory-Aware Direct Preference Alignment for Video Diffusion Transformers
summary: Efficiently aligning large-scale video diffusion models with human intent requires a scalable and trajectory-aware pathway that bridges the inherent discrepancy between training noise distributions and practical inference trajectories. While existing paradigms such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) attempt to address this, they are often hindered by either reliance on bias-prone, complex reward models or suboptimal timestep sampling. In this paper, we propose Diffusion-APO (Aligned Preference Optimization), a trajectory-aware algorithm that resolves this misalignment by synchronizing training noise with inference-time denoising paths to maximize gradient signal efficacy. To translate this algorithmic innovation into a practical solution, we introduce a unified and modular RLHF framework that integrates online ranking, half-online anchoring, offline refinement, and distillation-aware drift correction. This framework enables flexible, multi-stage preference alignment across diverse data and computational constraints without relying on scalar-reward-based policy gradients. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that Diffusion-APO consistently outperforms standard baselines in visual quality and instruction following, while effectively preserving generative fidelity during model acceleration, providing a robust, end-to-end pathway for scalable video diffusion alignment.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-APO is a trajectory-aware preference optimization algorithm that synchronizes training noise with inference-time denoising paths to align large-scale video diffusion models with human intent, outperforming DPO/GRPO baselines without relying on reward models.
AI.motivation: Existing alignment methods (DPO, GRPO) for large-scale video diffusion models suffer from bias-prone reward models and suboptimal timestep sampling, creating a discrepancy between training noise distributions and practical inference trajectories.
AI.method: Proposes Diffusion-APO (Aligned Preference Optimization), a trajectory-aware algorithm that synchronizes training noise with inference-time denoising paths. Built on a unified RLHF framework with four components: online ranking, half-online anchoring, offline refinement, and distillation-aware drift correction.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms standard baselines in visual quality and instruction following while preserving generative fidelity during model acceleration.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-APO provides a robust, end-to-end pathway for scalable video diffusion alignment without relying on scalar-reward-based policy gradients.

title: InterLV-Search: Benchmarking Interleaved Multimodal Agentic Search
summary: Existing benchmarks for multimodal agentic search evaluate multimodal search and visual browsing, but visual evidence is either confined to the input or treated as an answer endpoint rather than part of an interleaved search trajectory. We introduce \textbf{InterLV-Search}, a benchmark for Interleaved Language-Vision Agentic Search, in which textual and visual evidence is repeatedly used to condition later search. It contains 2,061 examples across three levels: active visual evidence seeking, controlled offline interleaved multimodal search, and open-web interleaved multimodal search. Beyond existing benchmarks, it also includes multimodal multi-branch samples that involve comparison between multiple entities during the evidence search. We construct Level 1 and Level 2 with automated pipelines and Level 3 with a machine-led, human-supervised open-web pipeline. We further provide InterLV-Agent for standardized tool use, trajectory logging, and evaluation. Experiments on proprietary and open-source multimodal agents show that current systems remain far from solving interleaved multimodal search, with the best model below 50% overall accuracy, highlighting challenges in visual evidence seeking, search control, and multimodal evidence integration. We release the benchmark data and evaluation code at https://github.com/hbhalpha/InterLV-Search-Bench

AI.tldr: InterLV-Search is a new benchmark for interleaved language-vision agentic search where visual and textual evidence alternates throughout search trajectories, with 2,061 examples across three difficulty levels. Current multimodal agents achieve below 50% accuracy, showing significant room for improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal search benchmarks treat visual evidence as either input-only or as a final answer, failing to capture scenarios where visual and textual evidence interleave and condition subsequent search steps.
AI.method: Three-level benchmark: Level 1 (active visual evidence seeking), Level 2 (controlled offline interleaved multimodal search), Level 3 (open-web interleaved multimodal search). Includes multimodal multi-branch samples for entity comparison. Automated pipelines for L1/L2, machine-led human-supervised pipeline for L3. Provides InterLV-Agent for standardized evaluation.
AI.result: Best model achieves under 50% overall accuracy. Current systems struggle with visual evidence seeking, search control, and multimodal evidence integration.
AI.conclusion: Interleaved multimodal search remains a challenging open problem; the benchmark highlights specific failure modes and provides infrastructure to drive future research.

title: Hierarchical Dual-Subspace Decoupling for Continual Learning in Vision-Language Models
summary: Class-incremental learning aims to continuously acquire new knowledge while preserving previously learned information, thereby mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Existing methods primarily restrict parameter updates but often overlook their structural properties in high-dimensional spaces. From a subspace perspective, updates induced by different tasks tend to lie in multiple overlapping low-rank subspaces, leading to cross-task subspace interference and severe forgetting. To address this issue, we propose HDSD, a Hierarchical Dual-Subspace Decoupling framework for continual learning in vision-language models. Specifically, we introduce a lightweight Feature Modulation Module (FMM) that explicitly decomposes the parameter space into general and task-specific subspaces. Building on this design, we develop two complementary components. First, a General Fusion Module (GFM) evaluates relative parameter changes across tasks and uses an adaptive threshold to capture stable and transferable knowledge. Second, a Hierarchical Learning Module (HLM) performs structured parameter decomposition via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and uses a scaling mechanism to constrain updates within distinct subspace scales. Together, these designs reduce subspace interference and parameter drift. Extensive experiments on conventional benchmarks show that HDSD achieves state-of-the-art results.

AI.tldr: HDSD is a hierarchical dual-subspace decoupling framework for class-incremental learning that reduces cross-task subspace interference by decomposing parameters into general and task-specific subspaces via Feature Modulation, General Fusion, and Hierarchical Learning Modules, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Existing class-incremental learning methods restrict parameter updates but overlook their structural properties in high-dimensional spaces. Parameter updates from different tasks lie in overlapping low-rank subspaces, causing cross-task subspace interference and catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: HDSD introduces a Feature Modulation Module (FMM) to decompose parameter space into general and task-specific subspaces, a General Fusion Module (GFM) for adaptive threshold-based knowledge transfer, and a Hierarchical Learning Module (HLM) for SVD-based structured decomposition with scaling constraints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on conventional benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: HDSD effectively reduces subspace interference and parameter drift in continual learning, establishing a new SOTA for vision-language models in class-incremental settings.

title: Implicit Preference Alignment for Human Image Animation
summary: Human image animation has witnessed significant advancements, yet generating high-fidelity hand motions remains a persistent challenge due to their high degrees of freedom and motion complexity. While reinforcement learning from human feedback, particularly direct preference optimization, offers a potential solution, it necessitates the construction of strict preference pairs. However, curating such pairs for dynamic hand regions is prohibitively expensive and often impractical due to frame-wise inconsistencies. In this paper, we propose Implicit Preference Alignment (IPA), a data-efficient post-training framework that eliminates the need for paired preference data. Theoretically grounded in implicit reward maximization, IPA aligns the model by maximizing the likelihood of self-generated high-quality samples while penalizing deviations from the pretrained prior. Furthermore, we introduce a Hand-Aware Local Optimization mechanism to explicitly steer the alignment process toward hand regions. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves effective preference optimization to enhance hand generation quality, while significantly lowering the barrier for constructing preference data. Codes are released at https://github.com/mdswyz/IPA

AI.tldr: Proposes Implicit Preference Alignment (IPA), a data-efficient post-training framework that improves hand motion quality in human image animation without requiring expensive paired preference data, using implicit reward maximization and hand-aware local optimization.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity hand motion generation remains challenging due to high degrees of freedom and complexity; constructing strict preference pairs for hand regions via DPO/RLHF is prohibitively expensive and often impractical.
AI.method: IPA (Implicit Preference Alignment) uses implicit reward maximization to align model by maximizing likelihood of self-generated high-quality samples while penalizing deviations from pretrained prior, plus a Hand-Aware Local Optimization mechanism focusing on hand regions.
AI.result: Achieves effective preference optimization for enhanced hand generation quality while significantly lowering the barrier for constructing preference data.
AI.conclusion: IPA provides a practical, data-efficient solution for improving hand motion quality in human image animation, eliminating the need for expensive paired preference data.

title: Probabilistic Object Detection with Conformal Prediction
summary: Conformal Prediction (CP) is a distribution-free method for constructing prediction sets with marginal finite-sample coverage guarantees, making it a suitable framework for reliable uncertainty quantification in safety-critical object detection. However, object detection introduces structured multi-output predictions, complicating the application of classical CP theory developed for single outputs. In addition, standard, unscaled CP produces fixed-width prediction intervals across inputs, leading to unnecessary width for low-uncertainty predictions. While scaled CP addresses this by adapting the interval width to an input-dependent uncertainty estimate, prior work has neither systematically compared unscaled and scaled CP for multi-class object detection, nor integrated CP with a complementary uncertainty quantification method in this setting. We fill this gap by: (i) applying CP coordinate-wise to bounding box corners with a Bonferroni correction for box-level guarantees; (ii) scaling the resulting intervals using per-prediction aleatoric uncertainty estimates derived from a probabilistic object detector trained with loss attenuation, evaluated in uncalibrated and two calibrated variants; (iii) extending to a two-step pipeline that constructs prediction sets for the class using RAPS and conditions the conformalized bounding boxes on the predicted class set. Across three autonomous driving datasets (KITTI, BDD, CODA), including a cross-domain setting under distribution shift, scaled CP consistently improves interval sharpness over unscaled CP, achieving up to 19% higher IoU and 39% lower interval scores, without sacrificing coverage. Class-wise calibration further improves coverage for both variants with a negligible effect on sharpness. Together, these improvements yield more actionable uncertainty estimates for real-time, real-world object detection.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares unscaled and scaled Conformal Prediction (CP) for multi-class object detection, introducing a coordinate-wise CP approach with Bonferroni correction, scaling via aleatoric uncertainty from probabilistic detectors, and a two-step pipeline integrating class prediction sets (RAPS) with conditioned bounding boxes. Experiments on KITTI, BDD, and CODA show scaled CP improves sharpness (up to 19% higher IoU, 39% lower interval scores) without sacrificing coverage.
AI.motivation: Object detection involves structured multi-output predictions, which complicates applying standard CP theory (designed for single outputs). Standard CP also produces fixed-width intervals regardless of input uncertainty. Prior work lacked systematic comparison of unscaled vs. scaled CP for multi-class object detection and had not integrated CP with complementary uncertainty quantification methods.
AI.method: (i) Applied CP coordinate-wise to bounding box corners with Bonferroni correction for box-level guarantees; (ii) Scaled intervals using per-prediction aleatoric uncertainty from a probabilistic object detector with loss attenuation (uncalibrated + two calibrated variants); (iii) Extended to a two-step pipeline: class prediction sets via RAPS, then conformalized bounding boxes conditioned on predicted class set.
AI.result: Across KITTI, BDD, and CODA (including cross-domain setting), scaled CP consistently improved interval sharpness over unscaled CP: up to 19% higher IoU and 39% lower interval scores, without sacrificing coverage. Class-wise calibration improved coverage for both variants with negligible effect on sharpness.
AI.conclusion: Scaled CP yields more actionable uncertainty estimates for real-time, real-world object detection; class-wise calibration further enhances coverage reliability.

title: Mind the Gap: Geometrically Accurate Generative Reconstruction from Disjoint Views
summary: 3D vision systems are fundamentally constrained by their reliance on visual overlap: reconstruction methods require it for geometric alignment, while generative models use it to enforce multi-view consistency. This limitation is particularly acute in real-world scenarios such as distributed swarm robotics or crowd-sourced data collection, where capturing overlapping perspectives, both in terms of spatial and appearance overlap, is often impossible. We introduce Generative Reconstruction from Disjoint Views as a new paradigm, establish a comprehensive dataset, and propose specialized evaluation metrics for zero-overlap scenarios. Our benchmarking demonstrates that existing state-of-the-art methods fail catastrophically on this task, producing disconnected geometries or semantically incoherent reconstructions. To address these limitations, we propose GLADOS, a general, modular framework that operates through three stages: (1) Generative Bridging, where foundation models synthesize intermediate perspectives to connect disjoint inputs; (2) Robust Coarse 3D Reconstruction, that establish coarse geometric scaffold via global alignment which absorbs local contradictions from generative process; and (3) Iterative Context Expansion and Consistency Optimization to fill missing regions and unify the reconstruction. As an architectureagnostic framework, GLADOS enables seamless integration of future advances in generation, reconstruction, and inpainting. The source code is available at: https://github.com/gwilczynski95/GLADOS.

AI.tldr: GLADOS introduces a paradigm for 3D reconstruction from completely non-overlapping (disjoint) views, using generative models to bridge gaps and iterative refinement to achieve coherent geometry.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D vision systems (both reconstruction and generative) require visual overlap between views, which fails in real-world scenarios like swarm robotics or crowd-sourced data where overlapping perspectives are impossible to capture.
AI.method: GLADOS is a three-stage framework: (1) Generative Bridging using foundation models to synthesize intermediate views; (2) Robust Coarse 3D Reconstruction via global alignment that absorbs generative inconsistencies; (3) Iterative Context Expansion and Consistency Optimization to fill gaps and unify the reconstruction.
AI.result: State-of-the-art methods catastrophically fail on zero-overlap scenarios (disconnected geometries, incoherent reconstructions), while GLADOS successfully addresses this novel task.
AI.conclusion: GLADOS establishes a new paradigm (Generative Reconstruction from Disjoint Views) with datasets and metrics, and provides an architecture-agnostic framework that can integrate future advances in generation, reconstruction, and inpainting.

title: VIMCAN: Visual-Inertial 3D Human Pose Estimation with Hybrid Mamba-Cross-Attention Network
summary: The rapid advances in deep learning have significantly enhanced the accuracy of multimodal 3D human pose estimation (HPE). However, the state-of-the-art (SOTA) HPE pipelines still rely on Transformers, whose quadratic complexity makes real-time processing for long sequences impractical. Mamba addresses this issue through selective state-space modeling, enabling efficient sequence processing without sacrificing representational power. Nevertheless, it struggles to capture complex spatial dependencies in multimodal settings. To bridge this gap, we propose VIMCAN, a hybrid architecture that combines the efficient sequence modeling of Mamba with the spatial reasoning of Cross-Attention, and performs robust visual-inertial fusion and human pose estimation between RGB keypoints and wearable IMU data. By leveraging Mamba's dynamic parameterization for temporal modeling and Attention for spatial dependency extraction, VIMCAN achieves superior accuracy, with mean per-joint position errors (MPJPE) of 17.2 mm on TotalCapture and 45.3 mm on 3DPW. VIMCAN outperforms prior Transformer-based and other SOTA approaches while supporting real-time inference at over 60 frames per second on consumer-grade hardware. The source code is available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: VIMCAN is a hybrid architecture combining Mamba's efficient temporal modeling with Cross-Attention's spatial reasoning for real-time multimodal 3D human pose estimation, achieving SOTA accuracy (17.2mm MPJPE on TotalCapture, 45.3mm on 3DPW) at over 60 FPS on consumer hardware.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based HPE pipelines have quadratic complexity, making real-time processing of long sequences impractical. Mamba offers efficient sequence modeling but struggles with complex spatial dependencies in multimodal settings.
AI.method: VIMCAN combines Mamba's selective state-space modeling (temporal) with Cross-Attention (spatial) to perform robust visual-inertial fusion between RGB keypoints and wearable IMU data.
AI.result: Achieves MPJPE of 17.2mm on TotalCapture and 45.3mm on 3DPW, outperforming Transformer-based and other SOTA approaches while running at over 60 FPS on consumer-grade hardware.
AI.conclusion: VIMCAN effectively bridges the gap between efficient temporal modeling and complex spatial reasoning, enabling real-time multimodal 3D HPE with superior accuracy on consumer hardware.

title: Dynamic Mode Decomposition along Depth in Vision Transformers
summary: Recent work has shown that contiguous vision transformer (ViT) blocks (a) can be replaced by a linear map and (b) organize into recurrent phases of computation. We ask whether these observations coincide: does ViT depth implement approximately \textit{autonomous linear} dynamics, admitting a single operator $K$ applied recurrently across a contiguous span? We test this using Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), which fits $K$ from selected, consecutive hidden-state pairs and predicts $p$ steps ahead via $K^p$. On four pretrained DINO ViTs, we study the regularization, rank, and calibration budget required for stable fitting. For short spans ($p \leq 4$), $K^p$ tracks an unconstrained endpoint map to within $0.02$ cosine similarity on DINOv3-H/16+, while also recovering intermediate activations at each skipped block. At early cut starts, the fitted operators compress to rank $\ll d$ with minimal calibration data, and across tokens, \texttt{cls} is most amenable to linearization; both properties decay monotonically with depth. Yet this local fidelity does not transfer downstream. At the final hidden state, after propagating through the remaining blocks, an identity baseline becomes competitive.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether contiguous ViT blocks can be approximated by a single linear operator applied recurrently. Using Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), they find that for short spans (≤4 blocks), a linear operator K can accurately predict hidden states, but this local linearization does not transfer to downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed ViT blocks can be replaced by linear maps and organize into recurrent phases. This paper asks whether these observations coincide—specifically, whether ViT depth implements approximately autonomous linear dynamics with a single operator K applied recurrently.
AI.method: Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) is used to fit a linear operator K from consecutive hidden-state pairs, then predict p steps ahead via K^p. Tested on four pretrained DINO ViTs, analyzing regularization, rank, and calibration budget requirements.
AI.result: For short spans (p ≤ 4), K^p tracks unconstrained endpoint maps within 0.02 cosine similarity on DINOv3-H/16+ and recovers intermediate activations. Early layers show compressed rank ≪ d with minimal calibration data; cls tokens are most linearizable. However, local fidelity does not transfer downstream—at the final hidden state, an identity baseline becomes competitive.
AI.conclusion: ViT blocks exhibit local linear recurrence for short spans, but this linear approximation fails to generalize to downstream performance, suggesting the linear dynamics are not the dominant mechanism for task-relevant representations.

title: Multimodal Stepwise Clinically-Guided Attention Learning for Pathological Complete Response Prediction in Breast Cancer
summary: Pathological complete response (pCR) is a key prognostic factor in breast cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant therapy, strongly associated with long-term survival and treatment personalization. However, accurate pre-treatment pCR prediction remains challenging due to severe class imbalance and limited generalizability across diverse clinical settings. In this work, we propose a multimodal stepwise clinically-guided attention learning framework for pCR prediction from breast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), designed to address these limitations through medically grounded spatial guidance and multimodal integration. The approach follows a stepwise training strategy inspired by physician reasoning: the model first learns global discriminative imaging patterns, then attention mechanisms are introduced to constrain the network toward tumor regions, and finally clinical variables are integrated to refine decision-making. This guidance strategy encourages prioritization of task-relevant features, improving identification of responders despite their limited representation in the dataset. Moreover, grounding attention in anatomically consistent tumor regions reduces reliance on dataset-specific patterns, thereby enhancing cross-institutional generalization. The framework is evaluated through external validation across heterogeneous MRI cohorts. Compared to non-guided single-stage baselines, the proposed approach improves sensitivity while maintaining competitive specificity, and produces anatomically coherent attention maps that support interpretation of the model's predictions. These findings highlight the potential of clinically-guided multimodal attention learning for robust and generalizable pCR prediction in breast cancer.

AI.tldr: A multimodal stepwise clinically-guided attention learning framework predicts pathological complete response (pCR) from breast MRI by first learning global patterns, then constraining attention to tumor regions, and finally integrating clinical variables, improving sensitivity and cross-institutional generalization.
AI.motivation: Accurate pre-treatment pCR prediction is challenging due to severe class imbalance (few responders) and limited generalizability across diverse clinical settings, motivating a need for more robust and generalizable methods.
AI.method: A stepwise training strategy: (1) learn global discriminative imaging patterns, (2) introduce attention mechanisms to constrain toward tumor regions, (3) integrate clinical variables to refine decisions. Uses multimodal MRI and clinically-guided attention for spatial grounding.
AI.result: The framework improves sensitivity while maintaining competitive specificity compared to non-guided single-stage baselines, produces anatomically coherent attention maps, and shows improved cross-institutional generalization on external validation.
AI.conclusion: Clinically-guided multimodal attention learning is a promising approach for robust and generalizable pCR prediction in breast cancer, addressing class imbalance and domain shift issues.

title: Beyond GSD-as-Token: Continuous Scale Conditioning for Remote Sensing VLMs
summary: Remote sensing vision-language models (RS-VLMs) face a fundamental mismatch with natural-image counterparts: the same geographic object exhibits radically different visual evidence across ground sampling distances (GSDs) spanning multiple orders of magnitude. Yet existing RS-VLMs often discard GSD or inject it as a discrete text token, forcing a single static parameter set to absorb the entire scale spectrum. We introduce ScaleEarth, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework built on Qwen3-VL that treats GSD as a continuous conditioning variable governing the model's computation path. At its core, CS-HLoRA (Continuous Scale-Conditioned Hyper-LoRA) modulates the LoRA low-rank subspace through a GSD-driven gate, enabling the model to dynamically route computation by physical scale. To remove reliance on sensor metadata at deployment, we pair CS-HLoRA with SSE-U, a lightweight heteroscedastic sub-head that predicts GSD and its uncertainty from visual features. To provide matching supervision, we construct GeoScale-VQA, a 1.5M-sample scale-layered RS-VQA corpus whose question-answer generation is conditioned on the same physical scalar that drives CS-HLoRA, forming a closed method-data loop. Trained with QLoRA on an 8B backbone, ScaleEarth achieves state-of-the-art results on remote-sensing benchmarks covering diverse Earth-system tasks, including XLRS-Bench and OmniEarth-Bench.

AI.tldr: ScaleEarth introduces CS-HLoRA, a parameter-efficient framework that uses ground sampling distance (GSD) as a continuous conditioning variable to dynamically route computation in RS-VLMs, achieving SOTA on remote sensing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing RS-VLMs ignore or simplify ground sampling distance (GSD) as a discrete token, failing to handle the radical visual differences across multiple scales of geographic objects.
AI.method: CS-HLoRA (Continuous Scale-Conditioned Hyper-LoRA) modulates LoRA subspaces via a GSD-driven gate; SSE-U predicts GSD and uncertainty from visual features; GeoScale-VQA provides scale-conditioned supervision.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on RS benchmarks including XLRS-Bench and OmniEarth-Bench, trained with QLoRA on an 8B backbone (Qwen3-VL).
AI.conclusion: Treating GSD as a continuous conditioning variable enables dynamic scale-aware computation, resolving the GSD-visual mismatch in RS-VLMs effectively.

title: Tracing the Arrow of Time: Diagnosing Temporal Information Flow in Video-LLMs
summary: The Arrow-of-Time (AoT) task, determining whether a video plays forward or backward by recognizing temporal irreversibility, is one humans solve with near-perfect accuracy, yet frontier Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) perform only modestly above chance. This gap raises a key question: do visual backbones fail to encode temporal information, or does information bottleneck lie elsewhere in the Video-LLM architecture? We address this question by isolating the vision encoder from the Video-LLM and tracing temporal information across the encoder, projector, and LLM. We find that video-centric encoders with explicit temporal modeling encode strong temporal signals, whereas frame-centric encoders do not. However, when video-centric representations are passed through a standard Video-LLM architecture, performance often collapses, revealing a bottleneck of temporal information flow. We identify projector design as a key factor: Q-Former disrupts temporal information, while a time-preserved MLP projection substantially improves the LLM's access to such information. Our layer-wise analysis further shows temporal representation dynamics across encoder layers. Guided by these findings, we build a Video-LLM with temporal-aware video-centric encoder, time-preserved projector, and AoT supervision, surpassing human performance on AoT$_{PPB}$ with 98.1\% accuracy, and improving broader temporal reasoning tasks by up to 6.0 points on VITATECS-Direction and 1.3 points on TVBench. Our results show that temporal reasoning in Video-LLMs requires both effective temporal encoding and reliable transfer of this information to the LLM.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates why Video-LLMs struggle with the Arrow-of-Time task. It finds that video-centric encoders encode strong temporal signals, but the Q-Former projector disrupts this information flow, while an MLP projector preserves it. By redesigning the architecture with a temporal-aware encoder, MLP projector, and AoT supervision, the model achieves 98.1% accuracy, surpassing humans, and improves other temporal reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Humans solve the Arrow-of-Time task with near-perfect accuracy, yet frontier Video-LLMs perform only modestly above chance. The authors aim to pinpoint where temporal information bottlenecks occur in Video-LLM architectures—whether in the vision encoder, projector, or LLM.
AI.method: The authors isolate the vision encoder from the Video-LLM and trace temporal information across encoder, projector, and LLM. They compare video-centric (explicit temporal modeling) vs. frame-centric encoders, analyze different projector designs (Q-Former vs. MLP), conduct layer-wise temporal representation analysis, and ultimately build an optimized Video-LLM combining a temporal-aware encoder, time-preserved MLP projector, and AoT supervision.
AI.result: The proposed Video-LLM surpasses human performance on AoT_PPB with 98.1% accuracy, and improves temporal reasoning tasks: up to 6.0 points on VITATECS-Direction and 1.3 points on TVBench. The key finding is that Q-Former disrupts temporal information while MLP projection preserves it.
AI.conclusion: Temporal reasoning in Video-LLMs requires both effective temporal encoding (via video-centric encoders) and reliable transfer of this information to the LLM (via time-preserved projectors like MLP, not Q-Former). The identified bottlenecks can guide future Video-LLM design for temporal understanding.

title: PolarVLM: Bridging the Semantic-Physical Gap in Vision-Language Models
summary: Mainstream vision-language models (VLMs) fundamentally struggle with severe optical ambiguities, such as reflections and transparent objects, due to the inherent limitations of standard RGB inputs. While polarization imaging captures polarimetric physical parameters that resolve these ambiguities, existing methods are constrained by fixed-format outputs and remain isolated from open-ended reasoning. To bridge this semantic-physical gap, we introduce PolarVLM, the first multimodal framework integrating polarimetric physical parameters into VLMs. By employing a dual-stream architecture and a progressive two-stage training strategy, PolarVLM effectively prevents physical misinterpretations while preserving general visual abilities. Complementing our architecture, we construct PolarVQA, the first benchmark for polarization-aware VQA, featuring 75K physics-grounded instruction-tuning pairs targeting reflective and transparent scenes. Experiments show that PolarVLM surpasses the RGB baseline by 25.4% overall across five evaluation tasks, with remarkable gains of 26.6% in reflection recognition and 34.0% in glass counting, successfully unlocking physics-aware semantic understanding.

AI.tldr: PolarVLM is the first multimodal framework integrating polarimetric physical parameters into VLMs, achieving 25.4% improvement over RGB baselines across five tasks by resolving optical ambiguities like reflections and transparent objects.
AI.motivation: Mainstream VLMs struggle with severe optical ambiguities (reflections, transparent objects) due to RGB limitations. Polarization imaging can resolve these but existing methods lack integration with open-ended reasoning VLMs.
AI.method: A dual-stream architecture with progressive two-stage training strategy, plus PolarVQA dataset (75K physics-grounded instruction-tuning pairs for reflective/transparent scenes).
AI.result: 25.4% overall improvement over RGB baseline across five tasks; 26.6% gain in reflection recognition; 34.0% gain in glass counting.
AI.conclusion: PolarVLM successfully unlocks physics-aware semantic understanding by bridging the semantic-physical gap, demonstrating that polarimetric parameters can be effectively integrated into VLMs.

title: Response-G1: Explicit Scene Graph Modeling for Proactive Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Proactive streaming video understanding requires Video-LLMs to decide when to respond as a video unfolds, a task where existing methods often fall short due to their implicit, query-agnostic modeling of visual evidence. We introduce Response-G1, a novel framework that establishes explicit, structured alignment between the accumulated video evidence and the query's expected response conditions via scene graphs. The framework operates in three fine-tuning-free stages: (1) online query-guided scene graph generation from streaming clips; (2) memory-based retrieval of the most semantically relevant historical scene graphs; and (3) retrieval-augmented trigger prompting for per-frame "silence/response" decisions.By grounding both evidence and conditions in a shared graph representation, Response-G1 achieves more interpretable and accurate response timing decisions. Experimental results on established benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of our method in both proactive and reactive tasks, validating the advantage of explicit scene graph modeling and retrieval in streaming video understanding.

AI.tldr: Response-G1 is a fine-tuning-free framework for proactive streaming video understanding that uses explicit scene graph alignment between video evidence and query conditions to decide when to respond, achieving superior performance on proactive and reactive benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing Video-LLMs fail at proactive streaming understanding because they use implicit, query-agnostic modeling of visual evidence, making it hard to decide when to respond as a video unfolds.
AI.method: Three fine-tuning-free stages: (1) online query-guided scene graph generation from streaming clips; (2) memory-based retrieval of semantically relevant historical scene graphs; (3) retrieval-augmented trigger prompting for per-frame "silence/response" decisions.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods on both proactive and reactive streaming video understanding benchmarks, validating the advantage of explicit scene graph modeling and retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Explicit scene graph alignment between accumulated video evidence and query conditions enables more interpretable and accurate response timing decisions, advancing proactive streaming video understanding.

title: Beyond Defenses: Manifold-Aligned Regularization for Intrinsic 3D Point Cloud Robustness
summary: Despite extensive progress in point cloud robustness, existing methods primarily improve performance through augmentation or defense mechanisms, while overlooking the geometric root cause of adversarial fragility. We hypothesize that adversarial vulnerability in 3D networks arises from a manifold misalignment between the latent geometry learned by the model and the intrinsic geometry of the underlying surface. Small, geometry-preserving perturbations along the input manifold often induce disproportionate distortions in feature space, revealing a misalignment between latent and intrinsic geometries. We formalize this phenomenon by developing a geometric interpretation of 3D robustness that links classical adversarial theory to the intrinsic structure of point clouds. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce Manifold-Aligned Point Recognition (MAPR), a framework that regularizes the latent geometry by aligning predictions across intrinsic perturbations. MAPR augments each point cloud with intrinsic features capturing local curvature and diffusion structure, and applies a consistency loss that preserves invariance to intrinsic, geometry-preserving perturbations. Without relying on adversarial training or additional data, MAPR consistently improves robustness across multiple adversarial attacks on both the ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN datasets, achieving average robustness gains of +20.02% and +8.58% on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN, respectively.

AI.tldr: MAPR improves 3D point cloud robustness by aligning latent geometry with intrinsic surface geometry via curvature-aware regularization, achieving +20% robustness gains without adversarial training.
AI.motivation: Existing point cloud robustness methods rely on augmentation or defense but ignore the geometric root cause of adversarial fragility. The paper identifies that adversarial vulnerability stems from misalignment between learned latent geometry and intrinsic surface geometry.
AI.method: MAPR (Manifold-Aligned Point Recognition) regularizes latent geometry by: (1) augmenting point clouds with intrinsic features (local curvature, diffusion structure), (2) applying a consistency loss to preserve invariance to geometry-preserving perturbations, (3) aligning predictions across intrinsic perturbations without adversarial training or extra data.
AI.result: +20.02% average robustness gain on ModelNet40 and +8.58% on ScanObjectNN across multiple adversarial attacks, without adversarial training or additional data.
AI.conclusion: Geometric regularization of latent-intrinsic manifold alignment is an effective and principled alternative to augmentation/defense-based robustness methods for 3D point clouds.

title: TraceAV-Bench: Benchmarking Multi-Hop Trajectory Reasoning over Long Audio-Visual Videos
summary: Real-world audio-visual understanding requires chaining evidence that is sparse, temporally dispersed, and split across the visual and auditory streams, whereas existing benchmarks largely fail to evaluate this capability. They restrict videos to short clips, isolate modalities, or reduce questions to one-hop perception. We introduce TraceAV-Bench, the first benchmark to jointly evaluate multi-hop reasoning over long audio-visual trajectories and multimodal hallucination robustness. TraceAV-Bench comprises 2,200 rigorously validated multiple-choice questions over 578 long videos, totaling 339.5 hours, spanning 4 evaluation dimensions and 15 sub-tasks. Each question is grounded in an explicit reasoning chain that averages 3.68 hops across a 15.1-minute temporal span. The dataset is built by a three-step semi-automated pipeline followed by a strict quality assurance process. Evaluation of multiple representative OmniLLMs on TraceAV-Bench reveals that the benchmark poses a persistent challenge across all models, with the strongest closed-source model (Gemini 3.1 Pro) reaching only 68.29% on general tasks, and the best open-source model (Ming-Flash-Omni-2.0) reaching 51.70%, leaving substantial headroom. Moreover, we find that robustness to multimodal hallucination is largely decoupled from general multimodal reasoning performance. We anticipate that TraceAV-Bench will stimulate further research toward OmniLLMs that can reason coherently and faithfully over long-form audio-visual content.

AI.tldr: TraceAV-Bench introduces a new benchmark for evaluating multi-hop reasoning over long audio-visual content (339.5 hours across 578 videos) and multimodal hallucination robustness, finding even the best models achieve only 68.29% accuracy, with hallucination robustness largely decoupled from general reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to evaluate long-range, multi-hop reasoning across both visual and auditory streams, as they restrict videos to short clips, isolate modalities, or reduce questions to one-hop perception.
AI.method: Built 2,200 rigorously validated multiple-choice questions over 578 long videos (339.5 hours) using a three-step semi-automated pipeline with strict QA. Each question has explicit reasoning chains averaging 3.68 hops across a 15.1-minute temporal span, spanning 4 evaluation dimensions and 15 sub-tasks.
AI.result: Strongest closed-source model (Gemini 3.1 Pro) reaches only 68.29%; best open-source model (Ming-Flash-Omni-2.0) reaches 51.70%. Multimodal hallucination robustness is largely decoupled from general reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: TraceAV-Bench reveals significant headroom for all OmniLLMs, highlighting the need for models that can reason coherently and faithfully over long-form audio-visual content.

title: SAM 3D Animal: Promptable Animal 3D Reconstruction from Images in the Wild
summary: 3D animal reconstruction in the wild remains challenging due to large species variation, frequent occlusions, and the prevalence of multi-animal scenes, while existing methods predominantly focus on single-animal settings. We present SAM 3D Animal, the first promptable framework for multi-animal 3D reconstruction from a single image. Built on the SMAL+ parametric animal model, our method jointly reconstructs multiple instances and supports flexible prompts in the form of keypoints and masks which enable more reliable disambiguation in crowded and occluded scenes. To train such a model, we further introduce Herd3D, a multi-animal 3D dataset containing over 5K images, designed to increase diversity in species, interactions, and occlusion patterns. Experiments on the Animal3D, APTv2, and Animal Kingdom datasets show that our framework achieves state-of-the-art results over both existing model-based and model-free methods, demonstrating a scalable and effective solution for prompt-driven animal 3D reconstruction in the wild.

AI.tldr: SAM 3D Animal is the first promptable framework for multi-animal 3D reconstruction from a single image, using the SMAL+ model and a new Herd3D dataset, achieving SOTA results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: 3D animal reconstruction in the wild is challenging due to species variation, occlusions, and multi-animal scenes, yet existing methods mainly handle single animals.
AI.method: Built on SMAL+ parametric model, the framework jointly reconstructs multiple instances using promptable keypoints and masks, trained on the newly introduced Herd3D dataset (5K+ multi-animal images).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on Animal3D, APTv2, and Animal Kingdom datasets, outperforming both model-based and model-free methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a scalable and effective solution for prompt-driven multi-animal 3D reconstruction in the wild.

title: FS-I2P:A Hierarchical Focus-Sweep Registration Network with Dynamically Allocated Depth
summary: Image-to-point cloud registration is often challenged by viewpoint changes, cross-modal discrepancies, and repetitive textures, which induce scale ambiguity and consequently lead to erroneous correspondences. Recent detection-free methods alleviate this issue by leveraging multi-scale features and transformer-based interactions. However, they still suffer from attention drift across layers and intra-scale inconsistencies, hindering precise registration. Inspired by human behavior, we propose a ``Focus--Sweep'' paradigm and develop a Hierarchical Focus--Sweep Interaction Module within an SSM-based framework to enhance multi-level cross-modal feature association. In addition, we introduce a Dynamic Layer Allocation Strategy that adaptively determines the iteration depth to better exploit geometric constraints and improve matching robustness. Extensive experiments and ablations on two benchmarks, RGB-D Scenes V2 and 7-Scenes, demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: A "Focus-Sweep" paradigm with Hierarchical Focus-Sweep Interaction Module (SSM-based) and Dynamic Layer Allocation Strategy is proposed for image-to-point cloud registration, achieving state-of-the-art results on RGB-D Scenes V2 and 7-Scenes benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Image-to-point cloud registration suffers from viewpoint changes, cross-modal discrepancies, repetitive textures, scale ambiguity, attention drift across layers, and intra-scale inconsistencies.
AI.method: (1) A "Focus-Sweep" paradigm with Hierarchical Focus-Sweep Interaction Module (SSM-based framework) for multi-level cross-modal feature association. (2) A Dynamic Layer Allocation Strategy that adaptively determines iteration depth to exploit geometric constraints.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on RGB-D Scenes V2 and 7-Scenes benchmarks, validated through extensive experiments and ablations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses attention drift and intra-scale inconsistencies, enabling more precise cross-modal registration than prior detection-free methods.

title: LithoBench: Benchmarking Large Multimodal Models for Remote-Sensing Lithology Interpretation
summary: Remote sensing lithology interpretation is fundamental to geological surveys, mineral exploration, and regional geological mapping. Unlike general land-cover recognition, lithology interpretation is a knowledge-intensive task that requires experts to infer rock types from various features, e.g., subtle visual, spectral, textural, geomorphological, and contextual cues, making reliable automated interpretation highly challenging. Geological knowledge-guided large multimodal models offer new opportunities, yet their evaluation remains constrained by the lack of benchmarks that capture lithological annotations, multi-level geological semantics, and expert-informed assessment. Here, we propose LithoBench, a multi-level benchmark for evaluating geological semantic understanding in remote sensing lithology interpretation. LithoBench contains 10,000 expert-annotated interpretation instances across 12 representative lithological categories, including 4,000 multiple-choice and 6,000 open-ended tasks organized into five cognitive levels: Identification and Description, Comparative Analysis, Mechanism Explanation, Practical Application, and Comprehensive Reasoning. We further develop an expert-in-the-loop, knowledge-grounded semi-automated construction pipeline, coupling multi sub-processes, e.g., structured geological image descriptions, to enhance geological validity and evaluation reliability. Experiments with multiple large vision-language models eveal substantial limitations in geological semantic understanding, particularly on higher-order explanation, application, and reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: LithoBench is a new benchmark with 10,000 expert-annotated instances across 12 lithological categories for evaluating large multimodal models on remote sensing lithology interpretation, revealing major limitations in higher-order geological reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks lack lithological annotations, multi-level geological semantics, and expert-informed assessment for evaluating geological knowledge-guided large multimodal models in remote sensing lithology interpretation.
AI.method: LithoBench contains 10,000 expert-annotated instances (4,000 multiple-choice + 6,000 open-ended) across 12 lithological categories, organized into five cognitive levels. Uses an expert-in-the-loop, knowledge-grounded semi-automated construction pipeline with structured geological image descriptions.
AI.result: Experiments with multiple large vision-language models reveal substantial limitations in geological semantic understanding, especially on higher-order explanation, application, and reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Current large multimodal models struggle significantly with knowledge-intensive geological reasoning, highlighting the need for further progress in this domain.

title: EggHand: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Egocentric Hand Pose Forecasting
summary: Forecasting future 3D hand pose sequences from egocentric video is essential for understanding human intention and enabling embodied applications such as AR/VR assistance and human-robot interaction. However, this task remains a highly challenging problem because egocentric hand motion is driven by complex human intent, exhibits highly dexterous articulations, and is observed under drastic viewpoint shifts induced by ego-motion. In this work, we introduce EggHand, a foundation-model-based framework for egocentric hand pose forecasting that unifies multimodal semantic reasoning with dynamic motion modeling. Our approach couples an action decoder from a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model, which captures the structured temporal dynamics of hand motion, with an egocentric video-text encoder that provides viewpoint-aware contextual information learned from large-scale first-person video. Together, these components overcome the brittleness of generic visual encoders under ego-motion and enable joint reasoning over motion, context, and high-level intent-without relying on body pose or external tracking. Experiments on the EgoExo4D dataset show that EggHand sets a new state of the art in forecasting accuracy, remains robust under severe ego-motion, and further enables controllable prediction via language-based task prompts. Project page: https://jyoun9.github.io/EggHand

AI.tldr: EggHand is a foundation-model-based framework for forecasting 3D hand pose sequences from egocentric video, using a Vision-Language-Action model coupled with an egocentric video-text encoder to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on EgoExo4D.
AI.motivation: Forecasting future 3D hand pose sequences from egocentric video is essential for understanding human intention and enabling AR/VR assistance and human-robot interaction, but remains challenging due to complex human intent, dexterous articulations, and drastic viewpoint shifts from ego-motion.
AI.method: The approach couples an action decoder from a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model (capturing structured temporal dynamics) with an egocentric video-text encoder (providing viewpoint-aware contextual information learned from large-scale first-person video).
AI.result: EggHand sets a new state of the art in forecasting accuracy on EgoExo4D, remains robust under severe ego-motion, and enables controllable prediction via language-based task prompts.
AI.conclusion: The framework overcomes the brittleness of generic visual encoders under ego-motion and enables joint reasoning over motion, context, and high-level intent without relying on body pose or external tracking.

title: Operating Within the Operational Design Domain: Zero-Shot Perception with Vision-Language Models
summary: Over the last few years, research on autonomous systems has matured to such a degree that the field is increasingly well-positioned to translate research into practical, stakeholder-driven use cases across well-defined domains. However, for a wide-scale practical adoption of autonomous systems, adherence to safety regulations is crucial. Many regulations are influenced by the Operational Design Domain (ODD), which defines the specific conditions in which an autonomous agent can function. This is especially relevant for Automated Driving Systems (ADS), as a dependable perception of ODD elements is essential for safe implementation and auditing. Vision-language models (VLMs) integrate visual recognition and language reasoning, functioning without task-specific training data, which makes them suitable for adaptable ODD perception. To assess whether VLMs can function as zero-shot "ODD sensors" that adapt to evolving definitions, we contribute (i) an empirical study of zero-shot ODD classification and detection using four VLMs on a custom dataset and Mapillary Vistas, along with failure analyses; (ii) an ablation of zero-shot optimization strategies with a cost-performance overview; and (iii) a suite of reusable prompting templates with guidance for adaptation. Our findings indicate that definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition performs best, while other methods may result in reduced recall. Overall, our results pave the way for transparent and effective ODD-based perception in safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as zero-shot "ODD sensors" for Automated Driving Systems, finding that definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition yields the best performance for Operational Design Domain perception.
AI.motivation: Wide-scale adoption of autonomous systems requires adherence to safety regulations tied to Operational Design Domains (ODD). Dependable perception of ODD elements is essential but challenging, motivating the need for adaptable, training-free sensing methods.
AI.method: The authors conduct empirical zero-shot ODD classification and detection experiments using four VLMs on a custom dataset and Mapillary Vistas, perform ablation studies on zero-shot optimization strategies with cost-performance analysis, and develop reusable prompting templates with adaptation guidance.
AI.result: Definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition achieves the best performance; other optimization methods may reduce recall. A cost-performance overview is provided across VLMs and strategies.
AI.conclusion: VLMs can function as effective zero-shot ODD sensors, and the proposed prompting strategies pave the way for transparent, safety-critical ODD-based perception in autonomous driving.

title: Breaking Spatial Uniformity: Prior-Guided Mamba with Radial Serialization for Lens Flare Removal
summary: Lens flares, caused by complex optical aberrations, severely degrade image quality especially in nighttime photography. Although recent restoration methods have made remarkable progress, most still rely on spatially uniform processing. They are failing to handle the region-dependent restoration demands of flare scenes, where saturated light sources should be preserved, flare artifacts removed, and background details recovered. To address this challenge, we propose DeflareMambav2, a prior-guided Mamba framework for lens flare removal. Specifically, we introduce a Flare Prior Network (FPN) to estimate flare priors and guide adaptive restoration. Besides, a novel radial serialization strategy breaks spatially homogeneous processing by performing flare-aware targeted sampling, and better supports long-range modeling in State Space Models (SSMs). Based on these priors, the backbone adopts a dual-level adaptive scheme. It explicitly preserves light-source regions to avoid over-processing, and applies curriculum-based restoration to the remaining contaminated areas while calibrating restoration intensity at the pixel level. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeflareMambav2 achieves state-of-the-art performance with reduced parameter burden. Code is available at https://github.com/BNU-ERC-ITEA/DeflareMambav2.

AI.tldr: DeflareMambav2 is a prior-guided Mamba framework for lens flare removal that uses a Flare Prior Network, radial serialization strategy, and dual-level adaptive scheme to handle region-dependent restoration demands, achieving state-of-the-art performance with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Lens flares from complex optical aberrations severely degrade nighttime image quality. Existing restoration methods use spatially uniform processing and fail to handle region-dependent demands—they cannot simultaneously preserve saturated light sources, remove flare artifacts, and recover background details.
AI.method: (1) Flare Prior Network (FPN) to estimate flare priors for adaptive restoration; (2) Radial serialization strategy for flare-aware targeted sampling that breaks spatially homogeneous processing and supports long-range SSM modeling; (3) Dual-level adaptive backbone that explicitly preserves light-source regions and applies curriculum-based restoration with pixel-level intensity calibration.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on lens flare removal with reduced parameter burden compared to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed prior-guided Mamba framework effectively addresses region-dependent flare restoration, outperforming existing approaches while being more parameter-efficient. Code is publicly available.

title: Aquatic Neuromorphic Optical Flow
summary: Underwater environments impose severe constraints on conventional imaging systems and demand solutions that balance high-quality sensing with strict resource efficiency. While emerging event cameras offer a promising alternative, their potential in aquatic scenarios remains largely unexplored. Through the lens of neuromorphic vision, this work pioneers the investigation of motion fields that serve as key media for agile underwater perception. Built upon spiking neural networks, we introduce a self-supervised framework to estimate per-pixel optical flow from asynchronous event streams, elegantly bypassing the long-standing bottleneck of underwater data scarcity. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves competitive visual and quantitative results against leading techniques while operating with superior computational efficiency. By bridging neuromorphic sensing and aquatic intelligence, this work opens new frontiers for lightweight, real-time, and low-cost perception on resource-constrained underwater edge platforms.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a self-supervised spiking neural network framework that estimates optical flow from event cameras in underwater environments, achieving competitive results with high computational efficiency while overcoming data scarcity challenges.
AI.motivation: Underwater environments challenge conventional imaging systems and demand resource-efficient solutions. Event cameras offer promise but are unexplored in aquatic scenarios, and underwater data scarcity is a long-standing bottleneck.
AI.method: A self-supervised framework built on spiking neural networks that estimates per-pixel optical flow from asynchronous event streams, bypassing the need for labeled underwater data.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive visual and quantitative results against leading techniques while operating with superior computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: This work bridges neuromorphic sensing and aquatic intelligence, opening frontiers for lightweight, real-time, low-cost perception on resource-constrained underwater edge platforms.

title: Towards Billion-scale Multi-modal Biometric Search
summary: Searching a multi-biometric database of a billion records for a country-level identity system requires pushing the limits of all aspects of a biometric system, including acquisition, preprocessing, feature extraction, accuracy, matching speed, presentation attack detection, and handling of special cases (e.g., missing finger digits). This is the first paper that gives insights into such a large-scale multimodal biometric search system, called Bharat ABIS, based on open-source architectures. The end-to-end pipeline of Bharat ABIS processes fingerprint, face and iris modalities through modality-specific stages of preprocessing (segmentation), quality assessment, presentation attack detection, and learning an embedding (feature extraction), producing a concatenated template of 13.5KB per person. We present a detailed analysis of the modalities and how they are integrated to create an efficient and effective solution for 1:N search (de-duplication). Evaluations on a demographically stratified gallery of 220 million identities, randomly sampled from 1.55 billion records in India's Aadhaar database, yield an FNIR of 0.3% at an FPIR of 0.5%, for adult probes (over 18 years). We also compare the performance of Bharat ABIS against three state-of-the-art COTS systems on a 20M gallery. Our system achieves a throughput of 100 searches per second on a gallery of 40M on a single server (8xNvidia H100 GPUs, 2TB RAM).

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Bharat ABIS, a large-scale multimodal biometric search system for India's Aadhaar database (1.55 billion records). It combines fingerprint, face, and iris modalities in an open-source end-to-end pipeline, achieving 0.3% FNIR at 0.5% FPIR on 220M identities and 100 searches/sec on 40M gallery using 8xH100 GPUs.
AI.motivation: To address the unprecedented challenges of searching a multi-biometric database of a billion records for a country-level identity system, which requires pushing limits across all biometric system aspects while maintaining efficiency, accuracy, and security.
AI.method: Bharat ABIS uses an end-to-end pipeline processing fingerprint, face, and iris modalities through preprocessing (segmentation), quality assessment, presentation attack detection, and feature extraction (embedding learning), producing a concatenated 13.5KB template per person for 1:N de-duplication search.
AI.result: On a gallery of 220M identities from Aadhaar, FNIR of 0.3% at FPIR of 0.5% for adults. Throughput of 100 searches/sec on 40M gallery using a single server (8xNvidia H100 GPUs, 2TB RAM). Outperforms or matches three state-of-the-art COTS systems on a 20M gallery.
AI.conclusion: Bharat ABIS demonstrates that open-source architectures can achieve production-grade performance at unprecedented billion-scale biometric de-duplication, providing insights for future large-scale identity systems.

title: OphEdit: Training-Free Text-Guided Editing of Ophthalmic Surgical Videos
summary: High-fidelity surgical video generation can greatly improve medical training and the development of AI, adapting these generative models for precise video editing remains a formidable challenge. Modifying surgical attributes, such as instrument tissue interactions or procedural phases is challenging due to the strict anatomical and temporal constraints. In this paper, we propose OphEdit, a novel training-free framework for the text-guided editing of ophthalmic surgical videos. Our approach leverages a deterministic second-order ODE inversion pipeline to capture Attention Value (V) tensors from the original video. By selectively injecting these stored tensors into the conditional Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) branch during the denoising phase, OphEdit rigorously preserves the intricate anatomical geometry of the eye while seamlessly mapping text-driven semantic modifications onto the video stream. Clinical evaluations demonstrates that OphEdit effectively handles complex surgical transformations, such as instrument swaps and procedural variations, with superior structural fidelity and temporal consistency compared to natural-domain video editors. Our work represents the first application of training-free video editing in the ophthalmic surgical domain, offering a scalable solution for generating diverse, annotated medical datasets without the need for exhaustive manual recording or costly model fine-tuning. The code and prompts can be accessed at https://github.com/ophedit/OphEdit

AI.tldr: OphEdit is a training-free framework for text-guided editing of ophthalmic surgical videos that preserves anatomical geometry via Attention Value tensor injection.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity surgical video generation can improve medical training and AI development, but adapting generative models for precise video editing in surgical domains is challenging due to strict anatomical and temporal constraints.
AI.method: Uses a deterministic second-order ODE inversion pipeline to capture Attention Value (V) tensors from original videos, then selectively injects these stored tensors into the conditional Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) branch during denoising to preserve anatomical geometry while applying text-driven edits.
AI.result: Clinical evaluations show OphEdit handles complex surgical transformations (instrument swaps, procedural variations) with superior structural fidelity and temporal consistency compared to natural-domain video editors.
AI.conclusion: First application of training-free video editing in the ophthalmic surgical domain, offering a scalable solution for generating diverse annotated medical datasets without manual recording or costly fine-tuning.

title: LAMES: A Large-Scale and Artisanal Mining Environmental Segmentation Dataset
summary: Mining operations are of utmost importance to the economy of some nations. However, such operations result in land-use change, very high energy consumption, and negative impacts on the environment, including soil erosion and deforestation. The mining process can impact an area much larger than the mining site itself. Adding to the negative externalities linked to mining is the fact that, in addition to government-sanctioned legal mining operations, illegal mining is widespread, including in various countries of Africa. The ability to monitor remote mining site activities can be useful, e.g., for the detection of illegal artisanal mining activities and their environmental impacts. An important outcome of such monitoring could include a better understanding of the interrelationship between mine facility attributes (e.g., mining types, processing methods, commodities, etc.) and their impact on the natural environment. In this work, we present a data set that contains 150 Large Scale Mining (LSM) sites and 870km^2 annotated area of Artisanal Small-scale Mining (ASM) sites. The metadata includes nine eminent LSM sections and 27 mining site attributes for each LSM site. We also discuss the data set's possible contribution to the research community, social and environmental consequences, and researchers' responsibilities from an ethics perspective.
